blocks.heading: A toolkit of four CLI generators that turn code, diffs, and specs into self-contained, visually-readable HTML — all built on one shared block→HTML rendering engine.

rows.value: Four generators ship as `bin/` CLIs: **visual-recap** (PR / commit / diff review), **visual-atlas** (this codebase-map), **visual-spec** (design-spec / RFC page), and **visual-doc** (general illustrated doc). Each emits one standalone `.html` that opens over `file://` — no server, no reader-side build.

Logic lives in `src/` and is consumed by thin entrypoints in `bin/`. A **rendering core** turns a typed `Block[]` into a page; **diagrams** and **code-intelligence** are shared services the products lean on. Two products *scan* a real repo (recap, atlas); two are *pure authoring* (spec, doc).

Every product is the same pipeline: build a typed `Block[]` → its assembler walks it, calling one renderer per block type → rendered diagrams and highlighted code get inlined → one self-contained HTML file.

primer.p: Each product pairs a block vocabulary with a thin assembler built on core's shared primitives — the renderers, lint, and highlighting. Only the doc CLI calls core's `assemble()` directly; recap, spec, and atlas each have their own assembler over the same parts. Read `src/blocks.ts` then `src/assemble.ts` and you have the pattern the other three follow.

primer.h: The output is a single self-contained file.
primer.p: Every generator inlines its CSS, syntax-highlighted code, and rendered diagrams into one `.html` that works over `file://`. Self-containment is a hard product constraint, not a nicety — it shapes how diagrams and assets are handled.

primer.h: Two products read code; two only author.
primer.p: `recap` and `atlas` understand a real repo — git diffs, Prisma/tRPC parsing, import graphs — through the **code-intel** layer. `spec` and `doc` take hand-authored blocks JSON and never scan. That split explains why code-intel exists.

primer.h: d2 is the diagram floor; the atlas is hybrid.
primer.p: Diagrams compile through the `d2` CLI to static SVG (with an optional editable-Excalidraw upgrade), and one palette colors every diagram. The atlas itself is *hybrid*: a mechanical scan drafts JSON, humans enrich the prose, and the committed JSON re-renders with no scan.

blocks.title: The domain map
blocks.intro: Six domains in three tiers. A **foundation** (`diagrams`) and an **engine** (`core`) sit at the bottom; an **intelligence** layer (`code-intel`) reads real repos; three **surface** products stack on top. Arrows are *depends on* — and `atlas` and `authoring` share `recap`'s review shell (its css, viewer script, and section helpers).

diagram.mermaid:
graph LR
  core["core · Block to HTML engine"]
  diagrams["diagrams · d2 to SVG"]
  codeintel["code-intel · parse repos"]
  recap["recap · diff review"]
  atlas["atlas · this map"]
  authoring["authoring · spec + doc"]
  core --> diagrams
  codeintel --> diagrams
  recap --> core
  recap --> codeintel
  recap --> diagrams
  atlas --> core
  atlas --> codeintel
  atlas -->|shares shell| recap
  authoring --> core
  authoring -->|shares shell| recap
  classDef changed fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f08c00,color:#1b1b1b,stroke-width:2px;
  class core changed;
diagram.caption: Read bottom-up: `diagrams` and `core` are shared infrastructure; `code-intel` reads real repos for the scanning products; `recap` / `atlas` / `authoring` are the user-facing CLIs. `core` is highlighted because all four products build on its `Block` primitives and renderers.

blocks.title: Spine
blocks.intro: The one flow worth memorizing — it is identical across all four generators. A product builds a typed `Block[]`; its assembler walks it and dispatches one renderer per block type; diagram and code blocks get compiled and inlined; the result is a single self-contained HTML file.

diagram.caption: What differs between products is only the *left edge* — how the `Block[]` is gathered (a git diff, a repo scan, or hand-authored JSON). From the assembler rightward, the parts are shared: core's renderers, highlight, and lint.

blocks.title: The 6 domains
blocks.intro: Each domain owns one slice of the toolkit. The bottom two are shared infrastructure; `code-intel` is the repo-reading layer; the top three are the user-facing products. Every tile links to a full deep-dive page.

tiles.name: core
tiles.purpose: The shared block→HTML rendering engine. Defines the `Block` union and the renderers every product draws on, highlights code, lints, and stamps the output. `assemble()` is the doc CLI's assembler; the other products' assemblers reuse core's renderers, lint, and highlighting.

tiles.name: diagrams
tiles.purpose: The diagram pipeline: compiles d2/mermaid sources to rendered SVG via the `d2` CLI (with a visible placeholder fallback and an optional editable-Excalidraw upgrade). One semantic palette drives both the d2 prelude and the matching mermaid classDefs.

tiles.name: code-intel
tiles.purpose: Reads and understands real subject codebases: parses Prisma schemas and tRPC routers, extracts imports, builds dependency graphs, and diffs API surfaces. Stack adapters make the scanning products (recap, atlas) framework-aware instead of guessing.

tiles.name: recap
tiles.purpose: The **visual-recap** product: resolve a git target (PR / commit / branch / working tree), gather and rank the diff, build a `Block[]`, and render a review document. It also owns the **review app-shell** (css, viewer script, section helpers) whose look atlas and authoring share.

tiles.name: atlas
tiles.purpose: The **visual-atlas** product — the generator behind this very page. Scans a repo, groups modules into domains via `atlas.domains.json`, drafts JSON (never clobbering authored prose), and renders the two-level atlas. Hybrid: mechanical scan plus human enrichment.

tiles.name: authoring
tiles.purpose: The two pure-authoring products: **visual-spec** (an approval-focused design-spec / RFC page with its own block vocabulary) and **visual-doc** (the general illustrated-doc renderer over core's blocks). No scan — the input is hand-authored blocks JSON.
